Author: Martin ter Haak
Title: Best AI Coding Agents Summer 2025
Text:
Best AI Coding Agents Summer 2025
AI coding assistants are an essential tool in today’s software engineer’s toolbox. And then I’m not just talking about AI-powered autocomplete like the original GitHub Copilot or even AI chatbots, but full-on agentic coding assistants. Wherever you are on the spectrum — from actively collaborating with your AI agent or letting it loose without any hand-holding — the benefits are impossible to ignore.
Left and right, new AI coding tools are popping up frequently, and existing tools are getting significant upgrades all the time. The developments in the space are super exciting. But they can also be quite overwhelming to keep up with. This is what I’ve experienced myself and heard from others as well. How do you stay on top of the best tools currently available?
Ideally, you would try them all out. But experimenting with different tools during working time can be a challenge. Fortunately, I recently had some free time while between jobs to work on a couple of side projects. This was the perfect opportunity to give some of the tools a spin. In this blog post, I’ll be sharing my experience and providing a ranking of what I think are the best AI coding agents right now.
6. GitHub Copilot Agent Mode
I was one of the earliest and most vocal adopters of GitHub Copilot at my past company. So I had high hopes when I heard GitHub was releasing Agent Mode at the start of this year. However, it turned out to be quite a disappointment.
By the time I got my hands on it, I had already felt the incredible power that other coding agents convey. In comparison, Copilot Agent Mode felt dated.
While other AI agents tend to do more than asked, Copilot Agent Mode tends to do a lot less — usually too little.
For example, I would ask it to change the signature of a function and mention one call site that required updating. Then Copilot would get to work straight away and update only those, skipping nearby comments and other call sites. Sure, it’s fast, but also lazy and frankly, quite stupid.
Just like from a real-life assistant, you would hope an AI assistant to be smart, proactive and not take your instructions too literally. Copilot didn’t meet any of those expectations. Its coding suggestions didn’t respect any of the surrounding code or folder structure, and sometimes weren’t even valid syntax.
One time, I asked it to move test data fixtures to the top of a Python file. Copilot decided to move them right before the first imports, which is a literally accurate interpretation of my instruction, but to a Python programmer, obviously not what I meant.
Also, in general, the coding assistant is too careful. Sometimes, rather than implementing a trivial change, it asks for permission first.
There we also a couple of UX issues, but since I’m sure by now I’ve fully discouraged you, I’m not even going to mention them.
5. Windsurf
Windsurf is an AI-powered IDE that’s a fork of Microsoft’s VS Code. Because of that, it’s already a very compelling and snappy IDE. Windsurf then added some mighty AI features like multi-line autocomplete, jump prediction and agent mode (called Cascade).
Out of all AI tools, I found Windsurf to be the best at helping you write code quickly. Its suggestions save you lots of clicking and typing (instead, you repeatedly press tab-tab-tab). But it’s 2025 and most developers are not approving suggestions solely on a line-by-line level anymore. We have agents now!
Compared to other coding agents, the agent mode integrated in Windsurf isn’t very good (at least when I tried it two months ago — things move fast!). It sometimes required many turns to accomplish a single task, despite my guidance. One time, I asked it to use cosine similarity <=> from pgvector, but it pretended to be deaf and used L2 distance <-> instead.
That said, its planning feature was handy, and it did have a noticeable effect on the structure of the execution. The agent makes sure to collect plenty of context before starting. And it doesn’t go off the rails by doing other things than I’ve asked for, which is a rare occurrence with AI agents. Also, I liked observing its thought process because it helped me understand what the agent is about to do.
The downside is that creating the plan and iterating on it requires many steps before implementation can begin. This approach is fine for complex changes, but it also applies to trivial changes, which I could have handled faster myself. Also, I’ve seen the agent make basic mistakes like generating duplicate code or getting stuck in infinite loops while executing a plan.
However, what ultimately made it lose to Cursor, another VS Code fork I will mention later, is its lag in terms of features. When I tried it, it couldn’t yet refer to files (like @my_file.py in other agents). Also, unlike Cursor, which integrates with the PyLance extension by default, Windsurf didn’t, which meant that it occasionally generated invalid code. Though I was able to work around this by installing the BasedPyright extension.
Windsurf still has excellent potential as an AI-powered IDE, but the existing flaws prevent me from wanting to use it again in its current state.
4. Junie
Junie is JetBrains’ attempt at a coding agent. JetBrains had already been in the AI tools space before, with an AI-based autocomplete (like GitHub Copilot) and an AI chatbot integrated into their IDE. I’ve used both previously and wasn’t very impressed. However, I thought Junie was quite a viable contender with the other agents.
Starting with its biggest drawback: it is slooow. Sometimes it takes more than 30 seconds to complete a response. But in return, its suggestions were quite accurate. The reason I believe it’s slow is that it compiles a significant amount of context before starting and then also writes a detailed plan. Though I generally found the waiting time acceptable, as the result required fewer tweaks than with other agents.
That said, Junie tends to generate way too many files, like tests and documentation, when not asked for. Also, it sometimes hallucinates information in generated documentation. One time, I asked Junie a question, and instead of answering, it generated a script and TWO documentation files. Its eagerness to write abundant code is also made worse by the fact that you cannot confirm the changes. Junie just goes off and makes a lot more changes than you intended to.
In addition, there were a couple of usability issues: it’s not possible to refer to a file or terminal window using @, there is no option to automatically run a command (you always need to approve), and there was a bug where I lost the state of the conversation. Also, integrating an MCP server wasn’t as failproof as expected. I attempted to have Junie generate React code from a Figma file, but due to an issue with reading the MCP tool result, it ended up hallucinating all the content.
Junie still has some way to go (and I’m sure it will get there). Although other coding agents overshadow it, it remains a valuable complement if you prefer to work within your JetBrains IDE.
3. Warp
Warp got me pleasantly surprised. For those who don’t know, Warp is a modern terminal emulator that was initially positioned as a significant improvement over other old-fashioned terminals available. And it might well have been (I hadn’t tried it then). But it really stepped up when it started adding AI features after its initial inception.
Now it’s basically a terminal and AI coding agent in one. And as someone who forgets which shell commands to use all the time, I think that’s a wonderful combination. Switching between regular commands and natural language queries is very intuitive.
When you ask a query, the AI coding agent jumps in. And I found the AI agent quite impressive. It’s fast, intelligent and obedient. It asks for permissions before running a command and confirms changes to files. I like how it detects when a new topic has started and proposes a new conversation, keeping the context clean.
In addition, I appreciated how it runs the commands step by step. After each step, it would explain the output of the previous step, propose the next command and ask for confirmation. Unlike other AI agents, it doesn’t do too much upfront planning, which is actually a good thing for command workflows.
After it’s done, it provides a clear summary, which provides just the right amount of detail.
As a terminal emulator, its main use case is in the realm of shell commands. But it was pretty good when asked to make simple changes to scripts and config files.
Like other AI agents, it has a tendency to clutter README files and scripts with things you didn’t ask for. But it isn’t as bad as others like Junie. And because it’s so fast, revisions are quickly made.
In addition, I would like the agent to sanity-check itself by doing a web search, to avoid recommending out-of-date solutions.
Overall, I found the Warp agent a cleverly designed assistant. It was intuitive to use and proved to be of great help. It strikes a good balance of prompting users with essential questions (e.g. whether to index the codebase or start a new conversation, confirming changes) while keeping out of the way at other times.
If your job leans more towards a sysadmin or SRE role, I would definitely give Warp a try.
2. Cursor
On Cursor, I experienced the first high of seeing an AI agent generate, run and fix code right in front of your eyes. All it took was typing “please run the tests and fix any issues”, and after coming back later, I saw all my problems solved. I truly believed we had entered a new era of software development.
Like Windsurf, Cursor is a fork of VS Code with AI features built in. The features across both IDEs are very similar. What gives Cursor the edge, though, is that it is all implemented slightly better. As mentioned earlier, Windsurf suffered from a couple of flaws that made it more awkward to work with, whereas Cursor feels like smooth sailing in most but the trickiest situations.
To be fair, when I first used it at the start of this year, Cursor still had a couple of glaring issues. The cursor would often jump in without having all the required context and would do more than was asked for. But it was clearly a time saver. Also, I wasn’t yet so comfortable with AI agents, so I vibe-coded myself into a mess of spaghetti code a couple of times.
But, with more experience and improvements on the Cursor side, I noticed sometime in July that working with Cursor was both fast and effective. By that time, they had implemented planning, which meant that Cursor would first explore the existing code a bit more and prepare a list of todos. This helped a lot both in understanding what was happening and in increasing the quality of the result.
However, the AI monologue can at times be too verbose. Like describing a simple change in a lot of detail, even though it’s obvious to a more experienced engineer.
Overall, Cursor is a great AI tool. There is a reason why it’s the most popular tool at my current company. Being VS Code-based means it’s already an excellent IDE (though I’m more a JetBrains guy myself). And with all the AI features doubly or triply more so.
1. Claude Code
Okay, so Claude Code really surprised me, as I wasn’t expecting to enjoy working with a pure terminal-based tool so much. Unlike the other AI tools mentioned above, it doesn’t come within a shiny GUI (though I’m aware there are some plugins now that add some level of integration). As someone with mixed feelings about terminal UIs, I thought that Anthropic, as the creator, did an impressive job at building a tool that feels as polished as graphical UIs.
The UI is very clean and satisfying. Also, without a heavy IDE, launching it up one, two, or even four terminals is quick and requires few system resources too. As an added benefit, you don’t need to switch to another IDE to use Claude. You can simply have it running in another terminal window while you inspect the results in your preferred IDE.
Command execution is implemented well, too. For each command, Claude will ask for permission, or you can tell it always to approve that command automatically. At times, it felt overly cautious (like asking permission to run ls or find), but I suppose it’s good to be safe. Also, it isn’t consistently able to recognise a command as being the same as one you approved before, but with different arguments.
Similarly, for code suggestions, Claude will ask you to approve it, or give you the ability to turn on “auto-accept mode”. In this mode, it becomes a true beast, going across your codebase, writing new code or iterating on previously written code to accomplish the task.
Initially, I was still a bit wary of the auto-accept feature, but I gained so much trust in Claude that I eventually started doing multiple refactorings all in parallel or even left it running while I went for lunch. It isn’t always exactly as I want it when I come back, but after a couple more nudges, it usually gets there. It truly makes me feel like an AI overlord.
If you have already used other AI agents, then this doesn’t sound too impressive. All the aforementioned tools have agent mode now. But Claude is both faster and smarter. It strikes a good balance between upfront planning and getting to work. Also, it’s obedient when it needs to be and reads between the lines when you’ve given it a vague prompt (of course, it still benefits massively from clear instructions). I rarely felt frustrated with Claude.
Also, for power users, Claude is a great match. It has support for additional context through CLAUDE.md files sprinkled throughout the codebase, along with custom agents for various tasks (e.g. PR review, security audit, UX, etc.) and a whole host of settings you can control.
It isn’t perfect, though. Claude would benefit from a bit more looking around before starting, as a few ls commands should be quick to do. Also, its preference to make local changes sometimes goes too far, for example, by putting import statements in the middle of functions rather than at the top of the file.
Another issue is that Claude Code is very eager to write new code rather than to simplify existing code. At one moment, I had three very similar testing scripts after giving correcting instructions, while I wanted just to modify one script. Also, in general, it can get lost in messy codebases or with vague prompts. But to be fair, these criticisms apply to every coding agent I tried.
Finally, I must mention its high price. Initially, I used the Anthropic API plan, which I wouldn’t recommend because it charges by usage, and if you forget to set a limit, costs can skyrocket. Use the individual Claude Pro plan instead. It’s still $17 a month and has a sliding window usage quota. For small AI-assisted tasks, this is enough. But if you’re like me and want to let Claude lose on your codebase, then the max plan of $100 is better to avoid hitting the usage quotas.
This is a lot, but I’d argue that it’s worth it for the significant productivity boost and overall enjoyment it provides.
Verdict
In the case you scrolled all the way to the bottom to read the verdict, here it is.
If VS Code is your preferred IDE and you want all the AI features you can get your hands on, then use Cursor. But if you want to stick to your current dev setup and need just a really good AI agent (or a couple) on the side, give Claude Code a try, and you won’t be disappointed. Or mix and match ;).
